metadata.title: BORDERING ON TOTALITARIANISM: PHILIPP ETTER’S DISCURSIVE SPACE

abstract: This paper clarifies characteristics of the discourse elaborated by Philipp Etter, a conservative Swiss Catholic politician, using his speeches from the 1930s. During 1933 to 1939, Etter, a State Councilor from the canton of Zug, was elected to Federal Councilor and eventually declared Switzerland's transition to general mobilization as Federal President. This coincided with the period when Nazi Germany seized power and precipitated World War II. Most scholars have viewed Etter negatively as a Catholic conservative who sought to transform Switzerland into an authoritarian corporatist state that bordered on totalitarianism. This paper sheds light on Etter's thoughts by using text-mining methods to objectively identify the arrangement of key terms in Etter's speeches from the 1930s, while remaining unbiased regarding the ideology of the research subject. The analysis shows that Etter transformed from an authoritarian conservative to a defender of Swiss specificity within the framework of consensus democracy.

paragraphs: Switzerland maintained its independence and neutrality when fascism and Nazism swept through Europe. However, Switzerland was not free from the influence of totalitarian ideology during this period. In the early 1930s, amid the economic dislocation caused by the Great Depression and the deadlock of parliamentary democracy, a movement seeking an alternative to the free-market economy and socialism, the so-called "third way," emerged in Switzerland. This movement led to frequent emergence of ideological and political groups advocating for pro-fascist or Nazi governance when the Nazis came to power in Germany in January 1933. Since the names of most of these groups included the word "front," the contemporary situation of the time was called the "heyday of the front movements (Frontenfrühling)." The Nazis sought to infiltrate the "enforcement of standardization (Gleichschaltung)"-a pillar of their social and cultural policy-into Switzerland through pro-Nazi factions within the front groups.

The awareness that Switzerland was a target of Nazi cultural policies led to the rise of a patriotic cultural movement, the "Spiritual National Defense (Geistige Landesverteidigung)." This movement was motivated by the ideological and cultural upheavals and turmoil that followed World War I. It became the collective name for movements that sought to protect Swiss culture from "non-Swiss elements." The term was first used at the end of the 1920s (Mooser, 1997, p. 689) and was regularly used in Swiss national politics around 1933 (Sandberg, 2007, p. 210).

Until about 1933, American culture, not to mention Communism, was also considered "un-Swiss" from the perspective of intellectual national defense. However, with the Nazi seizure of power in 1933 and their overt Greater Germanist ambitions, the priority of the cultural movement shifted toward resisting Nazism. On December 9, 1938, the Swiss Federal Council sent the "Organisation and responsibilities of safeguarding and promoting Swiss culture" (commonly known as the "Cultural Message") to the Federal Assembly for deliberation, which elevated Spiritual National Defense to the status of a Swiss national policy. Its doctrine became known as the "Magna Carta of Spiritual National Defense" (Independent Commission of Experts Switzerland -Second World War, 2002, p. 85). Subsequently, Switzerland drew a line of cultural differences between itself and Nazi Germany, and implemented various policies aimed at the spiritual and cultural integration of its people.

paragraphs: A key figure in elevating Spiritual National Defense, initially a private-level movement, to the status of Swiss cultural national policy was Philipp Etter (1891Etter ( -1977)). He was a member of the Catholic Conservative Party, with Zug as one of its main strongholds. From 1934 to 1959, he served on the Federal Council as Councilor (Minister) of the Interior. Members of the Federal Council are elected by the United Federal Assembly, which consists of the National Council (House) and Council of States (Senate) by an absolute majority. The Federal Council has had seven members since 1848, and posts are allocated in advance according to the number of seats held by major political parties. In Switzerland, the Councilor of the Interior is in charge of cultural policy administration, while the Department of Justice and Police is responsible for domestic security. Etter thus led Switzerland's cultural administration from the Nazi seizures of power until the early years of the Cold War. People called him "Eternel" because of his popularity and longevity as a Federal Councilor in an era of great upheaval. 1Etter was born in 1891 in the Menzingen Canton of Zug, located in central Switzerland. About 45 years prior to his birth, in November 1847, the "Secessionist War" (political-religious civil war) broke out in Switzerland between the Catholic cantons, which rejected liberalism, and the Protestant cantons, which supported it. In a short time, the battle resulted in an overwhelming victory for the Protestant cantons. Following this, Switzerland was reestablished as a liberal federal state under the Constitution of 1848. In the Catholic cantons, which opposed the centralization of power in the Confederation, a "strong political, social, and confessional unity" (Milieu) was formulated on the basis of "anti-modern, anti-secular, and anti-Protestant principles" (Shimada, 2007, pp. 334, 337). Historian Ulrich Im Hof calls this "a ghetto with the color of a Catholic subculture" (Im Hof, 2001, p. 122). Menzingen was one of such "ghettos."
The Federal Council had been dominated by liberal Democrats since 1848, but they were disconcerted by the referendums initiated by Catholic Conservatives and began to compromise with them.

Consequently, a Catholic Conservative, Joseph Zemp (1834-1908), was elected Councilor in 1891. In the same year, Pope Leo XIII issued the official encyclical Rerum novarum, literally translating to "the new things"; it referred to the escalation of the conflict between capitalists and workers. Since the publication of the Syllabus of Errors (in 1864) by Pope Pius IX, the Catholic Church had completely turned its back on the secular world (Taguchi, 1984), but in this encyclical, for the first time in history, the Church declared that it would address the issues of capital and labor. This declaration meant that, in the realm of politics, the Catholic powers began to seek a third path between liberalism and socialism. The Catholic Conservative Party, a national political party, was thus established in Switzerland in 1912. Therefore, the period from Etter's birth to his entry into college coincided with the full return of Catholic power to politics at the federal level, outside of the "milieu" or "ghetto."

After graduating from a monastery school (equivalent to a gymnasium) in Einsiedeln in 1911, Etter chose not to pursue a career in the clergy. Instead, he worked in journalism as an apprentice editor of the New Zurich News (Neue Zürcher Nachrichten), a conservative Catholic newspaper. In 1912, he began studying law at the University of Zürich, but at the same time, at the age of 20, he became the editor-in-chief of Zug News (Zuger Nachrichten), the journal of the Catholic Conservative Party in Zug. He completed his studies at the University of Zürich in 1917, and was admitted to the bar. In the same year, at the age of 25, he was appointed as a pre-trial judge in Zug. This was a gateway to successful political careers for Catholic Conservatives. A year later, in 1918, Etter was elected the Cantonal Councilor of Zug, and in 1923, at the age of 31, he was elected the Executive Councilor of the canton. In 1927 and 1928, he became the chief magistrate of the canton. After a successful career as a local politician, Etter was elected to the Swiss Senate as a Councilor of States in 1930 and began his parliamentary career. He continued to edit Zug News during this period. His journalistic career was halted only when he was appointed as a Federal Councilor in 1934. During his tenure as a Federal Councilor from 1934 to 1959, Etter served as the Federal President in 1939President in , 1942President in , 1947President in , and 1953. It was therefore Etter who was president when the Federal Council, recognizing the inevitability of a German invasion of Poland, announced to the Swiss people on August 29, 1939, that Switzerland would mobilize all its forces to defend its borders. 2From the perspective of the European political landscape of the time, Etter's political career, which culminated in his becoming president and declaring a general mobilization regime, coincided with the rise of Hitler's political career, which led to the outbreak of World War II. Hitler first joined the German Workers' Party in 1919 and became its leader in 1920. He changed the name of the party to the National Socialist German Workers' Party (Nazi Party), then came to power in 1933 and ordered the invasion of Poland in 1939. Thus, Etter went from being a "milieu politician" (Zaugg, 2020a, p. 15) to becoming Federal President at a time when National Socialism was expanding its power on the other side of the border and revealing its territorial ambitions overtly to neighboring countries.

Etter was unusual as a Swiss politician in that he addressed his ideas to the public in many speeches and pamphlets. These ideas were not academic or systematic thoughts, but he was able to put them into practice in Swiss politics. Historian Georg Kreis described this duality: "Because of this post, Etter was perhaps the most powerful 'intellectual' of his time and at the same time the most intellectual among the politically powerful in Switzerland" (Kreis, 1995, p. 201).

Etter's political career cannot be viewed in isolation from the larger changing political environment within Switzerland at the time. The Swiss Federal Council is essentially a coalition government with ministerial posts allocated according to the number of seats held by each political party, and policy decisions are made through consensus among the Federal Councilors. Thus, as a member of the Council, a Councilor is responsible not only for his own department but also for the business of his colleagues' departments. Additionally, they were also responsible for the conduct of the Council and the Federal Administration as a whole. The Federal Council itself and each of its members, the Federal Councilors, are both called the "Bundesrat" in Switzerland. The Federal President, as "primus inter pares" (first among equals), has no superior authority over the other colleagues, who rotate among themselves in order of age. Consequently, the decision-making process of the Swiss Federal Council is characterized by compromise and coordination between parties. The decision-making in cantonal and local governments shared a similar structure.

This fundamental form of decision-making, called "consensus democracy," was established in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. As mentioned above, the background to Zemp's appointment as a Federal Councilor was a change in the Catholic Church's social policy. However, the Liberal Democratic Party also turned to the Catholic Conservatives to contain the rise of the Swiss Social Democratic Party, which was founded in 1888.

In July 1937, a labor agreement called the "Peace Treaty" (Arbeitsfrieden) was signed between trade unions and employers' associations in the Swiss steel and watch industries. This "Peace Treaty," which marked a compromise between workers and the bourgeoisie (capitalists), continued to be signed in other industrial sectors. This meant that the Social Democrats, who had been hostile to the Liberal Democrats and Catholic Conservatives, took a cooperative line.

Communism and Nazism were excluded from the amalgamation of these three divergent political ideals. The Communist Party was outlawed in several cantons after 1937 and banned at the federal level in 1940. However, the Federal Council did not outlaw the Nazi Party directly but rendered it practically inoperative through various laws and regulations. In short, the cooperative line in Swiss politics was based on the exclusion of Communism as the extreme left for Switzerland and Nazism, and the front groups as the extreme right. Thus, Etter's political career took place in a changing political environment in which liberalism, Catholic conservatism, and socialism moved from confrontation to cooperation. His move from "milieu politician" to Federal Councilor led to a change in his career from a position representing the local interests of Catholic Conservatives to a political position at the federal level that transcends partisan and local boundaries.

However, the Liberal Democratic Party also turned to the Catholic Conservatives to contain the rise of the Swiss Social Democratic Party, which was founded in 1888.

In July 1937, a labor agreement called the "Peace Treaty" (Arbeitsfrieden) was signed between trade unions and employers' associations in the Swiss steel and watch industries. This "Peace Treaty," which marked a compromise between workers and the bourgeoisie (capitalists), continued to be signed in other industrial sectors. This meant that the Social Democrats, who had been hostile to the Liberal Democrats and Catholic Conservatives, took a cooperative line.

Communism and Nazism were excluded from the amalgamation of these three divergent political ideals. The Communist Party was outlawed in several cantons after 1937 and banned at the federal level in 1940. However, the Federal Council did not outlaw the Nazi Party directly but rendered it practically inoperative through various laws and regulations. In short, the cooperative line in Swiss politics was based on the exclusion of Communism as the extreme left for Switzerland and Nazism, and the front groups as the extreme right. Thus, Etter's political career took place in a changing political environment in which liberalism, Catholic conservatism, and socialism moved from confrontation to cooperation. His move from "milieu politician" to Federal Councilor led to a change in his career from a position representing the local interests of Catholic Conservatives to a political position at the federal level that transcends partisan and local boundaries.
sections.preview_head: A key figure in elevating Spiritual National Defense, initially a private-level movement, to the status of Swiss cultural national policy was Philipp Etter (1891Etter ( -1977)). He was a member of the Catholic Conservative Party, with Zug as one of its main strongholds. From 1934 to 1959, he served on the Federal Council as Councilor (Minister) of the Interior.

paragraphs: Among his tasks as the Federal Councilor for Cultural Policy, Etter's foremost focus was the drafting of the "Cultural Message" and its political realization. This message was issued in the name of the Federal Council, and because of its consensus-based democratic decision-making, any Federal Council message is a joint work, a patchwork, or an intertext (Hayanagi, 2018). In fact, the message contains petitions and proposals from left to right, from the Social Democrats via the Liberal Democrats to the Catholic Conservatives. However, Chapter 4, "The Meaning and Mission of Switzerland," which articulates the conceptual core of the "Cultural Message," incorporates Etter's speech and political pamphlets, The Meaning of National Defense (Etter, 1936) and Spiritual National Defense (Etter, 1937)-especially with emphasis on and revision of the latterand brings his authorship to the fore. Thus, while the "Magna Carta of Spiritual National Defense" is not Etter's work alone, it is primarily based on his political and cultural thought. Considering the intertextual nature of the "Cultural Message," Etter collated arguments and images on cultural integration in Switzerland into a unified message. Etter's role in the formation process of the "Cultural Message" can, therefore, be described as that of an editorial representative and first author.

Spiritual National Defense was not primarily a monolithic movement but rather a convergence of ideas that were incompatible in principle but came together specifically for the purpose of resisting the enforcement of the standardization of the Nazis. Etter's role was to provide the logic and imagination for this convergence at the national policy level.

As confirmed above, the national and international political situation in and around Switzerland changed markedly between Etter's appointment as a Federal Councilor and the outbreak of World War II. Therefore, a static examination of the characteristics of the "Cultural Message" as a text is insufficient when discussing Etter's thoughts as Councilor for Cultural Policy. Hence, it is important to trace the interweaving processes over time. This will clarify the political ideology on which Switzerland drew its cultural "border" and tried to make it visible at a time when the country was being surrounded by totalitarianism.

sections.header_raw: Studies on Etter and the Position of This Paper
sections.header_norm: studies on etter and the position of this paper
paragraphs: During his lifetime, studies on Etter have been relatively affirmative in their assessments of his ideas and actions. This is because, during the Cold War, Spiritual National Defense had been regarded as an expression of the "Swiss" resistance against anti-democratic political systems. 3 For example, historian Edgar Bonjour published an extensive study on Swiss neutrality during World War II, commissioned by the Swiss Federal Council between 1965 and 1976. He criticizes Heinrich Rothmund, who, as head of the alien police, perpetrated exclusionary treatment of Jewish refugees, and Marcel Pilet-Golaz, who, as president, directed Switzerland's adaptation to the "new order" in Europe led by the Third Reich. In contrast, he portrays Etter as a politician who resisted the Third Reich as the nurturer of the Spiritual National Defense (Bonjour, 1970, Vol. 4, pp. 115-137, Vol. 6, pp. 31-40;Independent Commission of Experts Switzerland -Second World War, 2002, p. 30;Zaugg, 2020a, p. 20).

Etter died in 1977, a year after the completion of the entire volume of Bonjour's report. Since then, an increasing number of studies have taken a critical stance toward Etter, as the generation of '68 has come to play an active role in historiography and cultural history. The new generation of historians tended to support the thesis that Switzerland paradoxically transformed itself into a totalitarian state to resist totalitarianism from the interwar period until World War II. For example, the concept of "Helvetic totalitarianism," proposed by Hans Ulrich Jost in 1983, remains influential today (Jost, 1983(Jost, /2006, p. 761), p. 761). In this context, the "Cultural Message" is seen as an undemocratic and authoritarian cultural concept led by Catholic Conservatives. Further, it has been claimed that Etter wrote the "Cultural Message" on the advice of Gonzague de Reynold, a Swiss ultra-conservative of the time, and an admirer of Hitler and Mussolini (Mattioli, 1994, p. 244). Indeed, some, such as Kurt Imhof, have argued that the Spiritual National Defense is not to be seen solely as a conservative cultural movement, but rather as an "anti-totalitarian basic consensus," of various factions that had been initiated in fact by the left (Imhof, 1998, p. 374). Since the late 1970s, Swiss historiography and cultural history have tended to view Etter as a politician who advocated Spiritual National Defense as an ideology that transformed Switzerland into a system that could be called "totalitarianism against totalitarianism," if not Nazism.

In 2020, Thomas Zaugg's doctoral thesis argued for a drastic correction of Etter's image (Zaugg, 2020a). Previous studies on Etter have relied primarily on Etter's writings, which are available at the Swiss National Library, with the exception of the "Cultural Message," an official, publicly available document of the Swiss government. In contrast, Zaugg was the first to systematically interpret Etter's documents in the Cantonal Archives of Zug to reconstruct his "portrait" (Zaugg, 2020a, pp. 23, 353). Zaugg used the key terms "center" or "middle position" to describe Etter's political stance (Zaugg, 2020a, p. 167). For example, during the "heyday of the front movements" in 1933, when Catholic youth showed signs of being inspired by visions of a totalitarian renewal of the state, Etter acted an ideological mediator between Catholic Conservatives, who wanted to adapt to a moderate federalism, and the youth (Zaugg, 2020a, pp. 156-157). In his "Cultural Message," Etter referred to the Alpine massif as a spatial metaphor for Switzerland as a multilingual state. In a broader sense, the Alpine region overlaps with the four linguistic regions of Switzerland. By addressing this mountainous region as the center, the mediation of multiple linguistic regions through the middle position becomes a metaphor for the mediation of multiple political positions. When superimposed on this spatial image, the "Cultural Message" becomes even more convincing as a collage of partisan arguments.

However, "this middle position has remained largely unexamined academically" (Zaugg, 2020a, p. 157). His middle position makes Etter's discourse, in many cases, ambivalent in character. Consequently, studies that focus on only one aspect of his discourse tend to produce a biased portrait of him: "Etter often appears as a projection screen and a synonym for the bellicose and xenophobic zeitgeist" (Zaugg, 2020a, p. 23). This depicts the Federal Councilor for Culture, who led the "Spiritual National Defense," as a statesman who tried to adapt Switzerland to Hitler's "New European Order" and transform it into an authoritarian, collaborationist system.

In fact, the "Cultural Message" and also the texts of Etter's speeches and pamphlets of the 1930s are not monophonic and systematic statements of a political idea. They mobilize the resources of available social and cultural discourse in order to seek the greatest possible consensus on momentous political issues such as "the crisis of traditional democracy" and "the unrest of Swiss national identity." For this purpose, Etter not only quotes the logic and discourse of various domestic parties but sometimes even takes the discourse of his opponents, who have caused the crisis and unrest in Switzerland, and weaves it into the context of his own arguments. For example, he points to Switzerland's multicultural status as a country with three linguistic spheres to demonstrate that Nazism's policy of "enforcement of standardization" was, in principle, unacceptable in Switzerland. In doing so, he uses the Nazi term "living space (Lebensraum)," a German concept of expansionism, in a very specific way, and then says the opposite: "the [three] great spiritual living spaces"foot_4 (Bundesrat der Schweizerischen Eidgenossenschaft, 1938, p. 998). 5 An analysis that does not take into account the whole logic of Etter's argument would misinterpret this use of the term "living space" as evidence of a pro-Nazi stance (Hayanagi, 2022, p. 37).

Zaugg's attempt to "correct misunderstandings and one-sided readings [of Etter's Discourses]" (Zaugg, 2020a, p. 14) has been criticized especially by historians of the '68 generation. Jakob Tanner was a leading critic, who associated Zaugg's doctoral dissertation with the tendencies of a global backlash in historiography and regarded it as a revisionist reinterpretation of Etter (Tanner, 2020). In response, Zaugg published a rebuttal titled "The accusation of revisionism ends historiography" (Zaugg, 2020b). Since Zaugg's argument is based on a vast amount of archival material, to settle this controversy within the framework of historiography, critics have no choice but to refute it the basis of an empirical examination of historical material. In contrast, this paper offers a new way of looking at this controversy by using text mining rather than empirical historiography to reveal the structure of the discursive space generated by Etter's text. This approach provides an understanding of his discourse that is relatively free from the interpreter's cognitive and emotional biases. By limiting the analysis to materials that have always been accessible in the Swiss National Library, it enables quantitative confirmation of the structure of Etter's thoughts.foot_7

sections.paper_id: Hayanagi - 2023 - BORDERING ON TOTALITARIANISM PHILIPP ETTER’S DISCURSIVE SPACE
paragraphs: Research on Etter has historically been based on a limited number of texts. His speeches and correspondence manuscripts were not published for a long time. However, his manuscripts were transferred from his family library to the Cantonal Archives of Zug at the end of the 20th century and officially donated at the end of 2013. Etter's unpublished documents were made publicly available in 2014, but remained unorganized. Therefore, Thomas Zaugg (1985-), a graduate student at the University of Zürich, was commissioned by the archive to systematically organize the documents from 2014 to 2018 (Zaugg, 2018). In parallel, he wrote a critical biography of Etter for his dissertation, which was published in January 2020. Then, from June to October 2020, the exhibition "Philip Etter: The Eternal Federal Councilor" was held in the same archive. paragraphs: Morphological prototypes of nouns and proper nouns were extracted from these documents and analyzed. To classify nouns, the German version of TreeTagger was used, and only NE (Eigennamen: proper nouns) and NN (normale Nomina: common nouns) were used. The total number of extracted items was 3822. Structured topic model (STM) was selected for three reasons: (1) STM can provide a more complete picture of topics over time by taking into account attribute information of documents (e.g., the year the speech was given, the year when it was published); (2) STM can assign a single word to topics based on the context, thereby capturing the characteristics and diversity of each topic more effectively; (3) STM can clearly demonstrate the correlation between topics. Three topics were identified from Etter's speeches and pamphlets from 1933 to 1939, enabling significant interpretations in relation to previous studies. In contrast, identifying two topics resulted in an overly simplified explanation, whereas four topics were difficult to interpret, largely due to the small size of the applied data. The STM was repeated for several times. Due to the characteristics of the method, there were differences in the details of each analysis. However, because the overall trends were similar, representative results are presented (Fig. 1).

Topic 1 is "National Defense for the Freedom of the Citizens." This topic barely appeared in 1933 (Fig. 2). However, it came to the foreground between 1934 and 1936, when Etter put together his basic ideas on "national defense."

However, this topic was deemphasized during 1936-1938, probably to abstain from provoking neighboring countries by avoiding the phrase "national defense (Landesverteidigung)," which carries connotations of military conflict. The data from 1939 consisted of several speeches made before and after World War II. Thus, "national defense" once again came to the fore as an obvious and urgent issue, since the danger of an invasion of national borders by the Axis powers had become a reality.

Topic 2 is "Preservation, Promotion and Education of Swiss Culture." This topic came to the foreground in 1933, then receded into the background, only to reemerge strongly in Etter's discourse during 1937-1938 (Fig. 3).

As noted above, the Nazi Party came to power in Germany in 1933, and under its influence, totalitarian political movements were active in Switzerland. Etter countered 2. Topic 1: National Defense for the Freedom of the Citizens these movements by calling for a to Switzerland's unique political traditions and culture, from the perspective of Catholic conservatism. In 1937, Etter formulated the basic ideas of his "Cultural Message," which emphasized the external and internal of Swiss cultural identity through the terms "cultural protection" and "cultural propaganda" rather than "Spiritual National Defense." Owing to the decentralized structure of Swiss society, cultural policy has traditionally been the responsibility of individual cantons, but the question of how the Federation should support them is also a part of this topic.
Topic 3 is "National Order Based on Authoritarian Democracy." Here, authority refers to the Catholic Church (Fig. 4).

This topic peaked during 1933-1934 and then receded into the background. During this time, Etter repeatedly emphasized the contemporary importance of the tradition of Landsgemeinde, an open-air general assembly that was the original form of direct democracy in Switzerland. In Landsgemeinde, the results of the vote by various classes of citizen voters in the assembly were seen as a manifestation of God's voice, that is, the Catholic faith (Sekine, 1999, p. 23). The printed version of the 1934 speech was published a few weeks before Etter's election as a Federal Councilor on March 28. This reflects Etter's thoughts at the beginning of his political career.

sections.paper_id: Hayanagi - 2023 - BORDERING ON TOTALITARIANISM PHILIPP ETTER’S DISCURSIVE SPACE
paragraphs: Studies on Philipp Etter began in earnest after his death in 1977 in the fields of history and culture. Many studies were critical of Etter as a Catholic Conservative, partly because the main authors belonged to the generation of '68. They interpreted Etter's texts as suggestions for plans to revive pre-French Revolution democracy, or "democracy" based on the authority of the Catholic Church in Switzerland, or more specifically, that he sought to transform the state system in Switzerland into an autocratic state based on corporatism in the manner of Italian and Austrian Fascism (Jost, 1983(Jost, /2006)). Thus, the "Cultural Message," the magnum opus of Etter's thought, is seen as an undemocratic and authoritarian cultural conception led by Catholic Conservatives.

To examine the validity of Etter's view, this paper analyzes the constellation of vocabulary constituting discourses in Etter's speeches and political pamphlets, and their changes over time, using the STM text-mining technique.

The three topics found are "National Defense for the Freedom of the Citizens" (Topic 1), "Preservation, Promotion and Education of Swiss Culture" (Topic 2), and "National Order Based on Authoritarian Democracy" (Topic 3). In light of the historical and biographical information covered in the introduction, the relative proportions of these topics are assumed to be influenced by the changing political environment in Switzerland and abroad, as well as by changes in Etter's career. Topic 1 comes to the fore with the emergence of the crisis of the border invasion of Germany into the neighboring countries. However, during 1937-1938, the proportion of this topic declined as an invasion became a real possibility. This is because the term "national defense" has connotations of war preparation on the part of Switzerland, and Etter did not wish to aggravate the issue. However, in 1939, when war finally broke out, "national defense" reemerged as an issue of the highest priority.

Topic 2 suggests that protecting and Switzerland's unique culture and educating its citizens about it is in opposition to the totalitarian system. That is, Switzerland, which was originally a part of the three language spheres and sought to remain so, was faced with the difficult task of clarifying the political and cultural differences between itself and (among others) the German-speaking sphere. This challenge receded to the background during the outbreak of war.

Topic 3 most clearly reflects changes in the biographical stage of Etter. The political pamphlet Democracy in Switzerland from 1934 was published shortly before his election as Federal Councilor, at the end of his career as a representative of Catholic Conservatism in the canton of Zug. The speech from 1933 and this pamphlet construed his last position statement as a "milieu politician" representing the Catholic interests of central Switzerland. However, when he, as a Federal Councilor, began to make decisions based on consensus with colleagues of different ideological positions, the vocabulary associated with the authoritarian state, originating from Landsgemeide, quickly disappeared from Etter's discursive space. This did not necessarily mean that he abandoned his original vision, but, at least on a discursive level, no evidence indicated that he sought to transform Switzerland into an authoritarian corporatist state like Italy and Austria in the same era.

Yet, one could assume, as most previous studies do, that Etter's discursive space of Etter in the early 1930s is the expression of his "eternal" thought. If so, his discourse of the late 1930s is also that of a Catholic conservative in disguise, which can be interpreted as bordering on totalitarianism rather than democracy. The emphasis on the unique Swiss culture rooted in the cantons (Topic 2) and the call to defend the land of freedom (Topic 1) show, however, that Etter's discourse in the late 1930s was different from totalitarian thinking. In other words, regarding Etter's assessment as a politician, the results of the analysis in this study support Zaugg's arguments. Namely, in terms of the discursive space created by the speeches and pamphlets he published with clear intentions, Etter could be described as a politician who transformed himself into a politician who thinks on a meta-levelthat is, at the federal levelrather than remaining a traditional Catholic Conservative. The thought he proposed during his first federal presidency was still conservative, but he was trying to envision a state that was neither liberal nor socialist, much less totalitarian.

Since the purpose of this study is to test the validity of the arguments of previous studies, the analysis is limited to the materials contained in the collection of the Swiss National Library, which they primarily used. However, it is apparent that more data is needed to improve the precision of future analyses.
